skill.name: miro-automation
skill.description: Automates Miro board workflows through Rube MCP, including board lookup, item creation, sticky notes, frames, sharing, members, and connectors.
skill.summary: Automate Miro boards, sticky notes, frames, sharing, and item lookup through Rube MCP.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command execution and weak-crypto patterns, but review found markdown tool names, parameters, and examples rather than executable code. The confirmed concern is the intended network dependency on Rube MCP and Miro OAuth, which can create, modify, and share real Miro boards.

medium_findings.title: Third-Party MCP and OAuth Access to Miro
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to add the Rube MCP endpoint and connect a Miro account. This is an intended workflow, but it grants a third-party MCP path for actions against Miro boards, so users must trust the provider and verify requested permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly requires Rube MCP and Miro OAuth setup. The risk is elevated because the documented workflows can operate on real Miro workspace data, but this matches the skill purpose.

medium_findings.title: Board Sharing Can Expose Workspace Content
medium_findings.description: The skill documents sharing boards by email with viewer, commenter, or editor roles. This is legitimate functionality, but mistaken prompts or unreviewed recipients could expose board content outside the intended audience.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sharing workflow and role parameters are directly documented. There is no evidence of malicious exfiltration, but the capability can disclose data if used carelessly.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The external command detections are false positives. The flagged lines use markdown inline code and fenced examples for MCP tool names, parameters, and workflow steps; no shell command, script, or Ruby execution appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown documentation, not executable code. The repository contains only SKILL.md and a report file, with no runnable Ruby or shell script evidence.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Text Matches
low_findings.description: The high blocker and reconnaissance alerts are false positives from ordinary words, IDs, color values, and table text. No cryptographic function, system enumeration command, or local host inspection was found in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain descriptive text, parameter names, color examples, and quick-reference rows. There is no semantic evidence of weak cryptography or system reconnaissance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded External MCP Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill directs users to configure https://rube.app/mcp as an MCP server. This network dependency is expected for the skill, but users should treat it as a trusted integration with access to Miro actions.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL is explicitly documented as the setup endpoint. The concern is trust and data access through the external MCP service, not hidden malicious behavior.

content.user_title: Automate Miro Boards with Rube MCP
content.value_statement: Manual Miro board setup and updates can slow team planning. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Rube MCP workflows for boards, items, sharing, and connectors.
actual_capabilities: Find accessible Miro boards and retrieve board details by resolving names to board IDs.
Create new boards, sticky notes, frames, and bulk item batches through Miro MCP tools.
List board items with pagination, type filters, and cursor handling guidance.
Share boards with email recipients and verify board membership after sharing.
Inspect connectors and guide creation of visual relationships between board items.
Explain Miro coordinate, pagination, schema lookup, and rate limit considerations.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP and an active Miro OAuth connection before workflows can run.
Cannot bypass Miro permissions, organization policies, admin approval, or API rate limits.
Tool schemas may change, so the skill tells agents to search Rube tools before use.
Does not include local scripts, offline Miro processing, or direct API client code.

use_cases.title: Prepare Sprint Planning Boards
use_cases.description: Create frames and sticky notes for backlog groups, risks, owners, and follow-up actions.

use_cases.title: Map Workshop Outputs
use_cases.description: Turn workshop notes into organized Miro items with positions, frames, and visual connectors.

use_cases.title: Audit Board Access
use_cases.description: Find a board, review its members, and share it with the correct collaborators.

prompt_templates.title: Find a Board
prompt_templates.prompt: Find my Miro board named "Q3 Planning". Search the current Rube Miro tools first, list matching boards, and ask me before opening or changing anything.

prompt_templates.title: Create Sticky Notes
prompt_templates.scenario: Add simple content to an existing board
prompt_templates.prompt: Add five sticky notes to the product roadmap board for risks, dependencies, metrics, blockers, and decisions. Resolve the board ID first and keep the notes grouped near the center.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Workshop Frame
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workshop frame on the selected Miro board with sections for goals, ideas, votes, and next steps. Use readable spacing and confirm the item count before creating bulk items.

prompt_templates.title: Share and Verify Access
prompt_templates.prompt: Share the board named "Design Review" with the listed teammates as commenters, then verify current board members. Show me the recipients and roles before sending invites.

output_examples.input: Find the team retro board and summarize what actions are available.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists matching boards, identifies the most likely retro board, and explains that it can retrieve details, list items, add notes, or share the board after confirmation.

output_examples.input: Create a planning area with four frames and starter sticky notes.
output_examples.output: The assistant resolves the board, proposes frame names and note labels, confirms the layout, then reports the created frames and sticky notes in plain language.

output_examples.input: Share the roadmap board with two reviewers.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the board and email recipients, requests approval before sending invites, and reports the resulting access roles after verification.

best_practices: Search current Rube tool schemas before calling any Miro tool.
Resolve board names to board IDs and confirm targets before write or share actions.
Use bulk creation for many items and keep coordinates consistent to avoid overlap.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode board IDs across accounts or workspaces.
Do not share boards without confirming recipients, roles, and organization policy.
Do not create many individual items when a bulk operation is available.

faq.question: Does this skill require a Miro account?
faq.answer: Yes. It requires an active Miro connection through Rube MCP before board operations can run.

faq.question: Can it create new Miro boards?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents a Miro board creation workflow through the available Rube MCP tools.

faq.question: Can it modify existing board content?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide item creation, sticky notes, frames, connectors, and bulk updates when permissions allow.

faq.question: Can it share boards with other users?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes board sharing guidance with email recipients and viewer, commenter, or editor roles.

faq.question: Does it run local code?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed skill is documentation for MCP tool workflows and does not include executable local scripts.

faq.question: Why should tools be searched first?
faq.answer: Rube MCP tool schemas can change, so searching first helps the agent use current parameters and limits.
